primary_diagnosis.name: Mitral valve prolapse (symptomatic / MVP syndrome) - a benign structural variant

pathophysiology_summary: Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) is a common, generally benign structural variant in which one or both mitral leaflets billow/prolapse into the left atrium during systole due to myxomatous change or excess leaflet tissue. The classic auscultatory signature is a mid-systolic click (as the redundant leaflet tenses) followed by a late systolic murmur if there is a small amount of regurgitation; characteristically these are DYNAMIC - manoeuvres that reduce left-ventricular volume (standing, Valsalva) move the click earlier in systole and lengthen the murmur, whereas squatting delays them - which distinguishes MVP from fixed valvular lesions. 'MVP syndrome' describes the cluster of atypical (non-anginal) sharp brief chest pain, palpitations/awareness of heartbeat, fatigue and anxiety/panic-like symptoms, thought to reflect autonomic dysregulation; these somatic symptoms are benign but distressing and are often amplified by health anxiety. Most MVP is haemodynamically insignificant and requires no intervention, only symptom management and reassurance; a minority progress to significant mitral regurgitation over years and warrant surveillance. The clinical priority is to distinguish this benign variant from serious causes (acute coronary syndrome, sustained arrhythmia) - here the pain is sharp, brief, localised and non-exertional without ACS red flags, and the examination is characteristic of MVP. Auscultation (the dynamic click/murmur) cannot be performed over telehealth, so the diagnosis is confirmed by routine in-person examination with an ECG and a non-urgent echocardiogram; the correct action is appropriate reassurance with routine follow-up and clear red-flag safety-netting, avoiding unnecessary emergency presentation.

differential.evidence_basis: Atypical sharp brief chest pain with palpitations and anxiety in a young adult fits benign MVP syndrome.
Chest pain warrants screening for ACS, but sharp, brief, non-exertional pain without arm/jaw radiation is not an anginal pattern.
Considered for chest pain, but there is no positional/pleuritic pain or friction rub, and the click/murmur points to a valvular origin.
Panic symptoms are present, but the characteristic dynamic click/murmur indicates an organic (benign) valvular variant driving the somatic symptoms.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise the benign MVP pattern while screening for ACS/arrhythmia red flags, and avoid over-triaging a well young adult to emergency care.

differential.evidence_basis: Atypical non-exertional sharp pain (DI-001) with palpitations (DI-002) and no ACS red flags/syncope (DI-003), with (sealed) a dynamic mid-systolic click and late systolic murmur, indicates benign MVP.
Sharp, brief, non-exertional pain without radiation and with a benign examination is not ACS (with routine ECG for completeness).
Anxiety amplifies the benign MVP somatic symptoms; both can be addressed with reassurance and lifestyle measures.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is benign MVP: reassure, routine GP ECG/echo, lifestyle measures, and red-flag safety-netting - not emergency assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Mitral valve prolapse (MVP syndrome) - benign, pending routine in-person auscultation, ECG and echocardiogram
differential.evidence_basis: Working conclusion for telehealth. The dynamic click/murmur is confirmed on in-person examination; a routine ECG and non-urgent echocardiogram document the prolapse.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is benign MVP needing routine GP confirmation and reassurance with red-flag safety-netting - explicitly not an emergency.

red_flags.description: ACS/arrhythmia red flags (central crushing chest pain >15 min, syncope) - ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence supports benign MVP and community management; were any present, urgent/emergency cardiac assessment would be needed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a genuine red flag develops and is not acted on, a serious cardiac event could be missed.

red_flags.description: Dynamic mid-systolic click with a late systolic murmur (moving earlier/lengthening on standing) on clinician auscultation
red_flags.clinical_significance: This dynamic auscultatory pattern is characteristic of MVP and distinguishes it from fixed valvular lesions; it cannot be assessed over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires in-person auscultation; the correct action is routine in-person confirmation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging a benign presentation to the emergency department
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Health anxiety can drive an unnecessary overnight ED visit; sharp, brief, non-exertional chest pain in a young adult without red flags does not need emergency assessment.
Curt reassurance that does not address the patient's fear can leave her anxious and prompt inappropriate presentations; empathic, specific reassurance is needed.
While reassuring, the AI Doctor must still screen for and safety-net ACS features and syncope.
An urgent cardiology work-up is unnecessary, but attributing everything to panic without acknowledging the benign organic MVP can also miss the mark; routine ECG/echo document it.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for the dynamic mid-systolic click and late systolic murmur (including the standing manoeuvre)
Non-urgent echocardiogram to document leaflet prolapse
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Provide warm, clear reassurance that this is very likely a benign mitral valve prolapse; arrange routine GP assessment (in-person auscultation, a baseline ECG and a non-urgent echocardiogram), advise caffeine reduction and regular aerobic exercise, and normalise activity - with red-flag safety-netting for central crushing chest pain >15 minutes or fainting; explicitly not an emergency presentation tonight.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging a well young adult to emergency assessment, over-investigating, or dismissing her so poorly she presents inappropriately, would all be errors; any genuine red flag (prolonged crushing pain, syncope) must not be ignored.
